title: Do Wholesalers Pay Capital Gains or Ordinary Income Tax? The Complete Answer
excerpt: Most real estate wholesalers are surprised to learn they don't pay the lower capital gains tax rate — they pay ordinary income tax instead. Here's why, and what you can do to keep more of your assignment fees.
author: AIWholesail Team
tags: do wholesalers pay capital gains or ordinary income tax
metaDescription: Do wholesalers pay capital gains or ordinary income tax? Learn how the IRS classifies wholesale income and how to reduce your tax bill legally.
metaKeywords: do wholesalers pay capital gains or ordinary income tax, real estate wholesaling taxes, wholesale assignment fee taxes, ordinary income tax real estate, wholesaling self-employment tax, real estate investor tax strategy

sections.content: One of the most common questions new wholesalers ask their accountant — or Google at midnight — is this: do wholesalers pay capital gains or ordinary income tax? The short answer is ordinary income tax. And that distinction matters a lot, because ordinary income tax rates can be nearly double what long-term capital gains rates are. If you're building a wholesale business and want to keep more of what you earn, understanding how the IRS sees your activity is step one. For a broader look at how taxes fit into a growing operation, check out our [Scaling a Real Estate Investing Business: Complete Guide](https://aiwholesail.com/guides/scaling-real-estate-investing-business).
Why Wholesalers Are Taxed as Dealers, Not Investors

The IRS draws a hard line between real estate investors and real estate dealers. An investor buys property, holds it, and eventually sells it — usually for a long-term capital gain. A dealer buys and sells property repeatedly as part of a business. Wholesalers almost always fall into the dealer category because they are actively finding contracts, assigning them for fees, and doing it over and over again. The IRS looks at factors like frequency of sales, holding period, and your intent when you acquired the property. When you're flipping contracts every 30 days, there's no gray area — that's a trade or business, not passive investing.
The Holding Period Problem

Even if you close on a property instead of assigning the contract, the holding period matters enormously. To qualify for long-term capital gains rates — which max out at 20% for high earners versus 37% for ordinary income — you must hold a property for more than one year. Most wholesalers hold properties for days or weeks, not years. That means even when they do take title, they're almost always selling at a short-term gain, which is taxed at ordinary income rates anyway. The favorable capital gains treatment is simply not available to most people doing traditional volume wholesaling.
The Two-Tax Problem: Income Tax Plus Self-Employment Tax

Here's where it gets more painful. As a dealer, your wholesale income isn't just subject to federal and state income tax — it's also subject to self-employment (SE) tax. Self-employment tax covers Social Security and Medicare and adds 15.3% on top of your regular income tax for the first $160,200 of net self-employment income (2023 figure). On income above that threshold, you still pay the 2.9% Medicare portion. So if you made $80,000 in assignment fees, you could owe roughly $12,000 in SE tax alone before a dollar of income tax is calculated. This is the number that shocks most wholesalers when they file their first Schedule C.

Pro Tip: Elect S-Corp status through your LLC once your net wholesale income consistently exceeds $50,000–$60,000 per year. By paying yourself a reasonable salary and taking the rest as a distribution, you can legally reduce the portion of income subject to self-employment tax. Talk to a CPA who specializes in real estate before making this move.
What Counts as Wholesale Income?

Whether you earn money through assignment fees or double closes, the IRS treats it the same way. An assignment fee is the amount you charge an end buyer to take over your purchase contract. A double close involves you actually buying the property and immediately reselling it. In both cases, the net profit is ordinary income reported on Schedule C if you're a sole proprietor, or passed through from your LLC or S-Corp. There is no structure that magically converts dealer income into capital gains income — the nature of the activity is what determines the tax treatment, not the entity type.

items: Assignment fees: Ordinary income, reported on Schedule C or through your entity
Double close profits: Ordinary income, same as assignment fees
Earnest money you keep when a deal falls apart: Ordinary income
Consulting fees for helping other investors find deals: Ordinary income
Interest income from seller financing you hold: Ordinary income (separate category)

sections.content: How Flippers and Landlords Compare

If you're thinking about expanding beyond wholesaling, it helps to understand how the tax picture changes. Fix-and-flip investors who sell within 12 months face the same ordinary income treatment as wholesalers. However, investors who hold rental properties enjoy depreciation deductions, the potential for long-term capital gains on eventual sale, and access to strategies like the 1031 exchange to defer taxes indefinitely. Our [Rental Property Investing for Beginners guide](https://aiwholesail.com/guides/rental-property-investing-guide) covers how the tax advantages of buy-and-hold investing stack up over time. And if you're interested in recycling equity efficiently, the [BRRRR Method](https://aiwholesail.com/guides/brrrr-method-explained) is worth exploring as a complement to your wholesale operation.

"The tax code is not designed to reward people who flip contracts quickly. It rewards patience. The longer you hold an asset, the more tax-advantaged your exit becomes." — Common wisdom among experienced real estate CPAs
Legal Ways to Reduce Your Wholesale Tax Bill

You can't change the character of wholesale income from ordinary to capital, but you can absolutely reduce how much of it is taxable. Every legitimate business expense lowers your net profit and therefore your tax bill. Wholesalers often leave significant deductions on the table because they don't track their spending carefully.

items: Marketing costs: Direct mail, pay-per-click ads, bandit signs, cold calling software
Driving expenses: Mileage tracked when scouting neighborhoods or meeting sellers (67 cents per mile in 2024)
Home office deduction: If you run your business from home and use a dedicated space
Software and tools: CRM, skip tracing, deal analysis platforms
Professional fees: Attorney, accountant, title company fees
Education: Courses, books, coaching programs directly related to your real estate business
Earnest money deposits you lost on deals that didn't close

sections.content: Pro Tip: Use our free [Deal Calculator](https://aiwholesail.com/tools/wholesale-deal-calculator) to model your deal profits before you close, so you can estimate your tax exposure and set aside the right percentage before you spend your assignment fee. A common rule of thumb is to set aside 25–30% of every check for taxes.
Tracking Your Business Like a Real Company

The best wholesalers run their operations like real businesses, which makes tax time far less painful. That means using a separate business bank account, tracking every expense with accounting software, and reviewing your numbers monthly. If you want to understand which metrics matter most for your operation's health, our article on [Real Estate Investing KPIs: What to Track and Why](https://aiwholesail.com/blog/real-estate-investing-kpis) gives you a practical framework. When you're managing multiple deals at once, a solid [deal pipeline management](https://aiwholesail.com/blog/deal-pipeline-management) system also ensures you never lose track of where revenue is coming from — which makes reconciling your books at year-end much simpler.

If you're scaling up and bringing on team members to help find deals, make sure you understand the financial implications of growing your payroll. Our guide on [when to hire an acquisitions manager](https://aiwholesail.com/blog/acquisitions-manager-real-estate) walks through how to structure compensation in a way that's tax-efficient for both you and your hire. Markets like [Atlanta](https://aiwholesail.com/markets/atlanta-ga), [Dallas](https://aiwholesail.com/markets/dallas-tx), and [Phoenix](https://aiwholesail.com/markets/phoenix-az) have high deal volume, which means higher gross revenue — but also higher tax exposure if you're not organized. Our free [Cash Flow Calculator](https://aiwholesail.com/tools/cash-flow-calculator) can help you model your true take-home after taxes and expenses in any market.
What to Do Right Now

items: Open a dedicated business checking account if you haven't already
Start tracking every business expense immediately — even small ones add up
Estimate your quarterly tax payments to avoid underpayment penalties (due January, April, June, and September)
Consult a real estate-focused CPA to evaluate whether an S-Corp election makes sense for your income level
Set aside 25–30% of every assignment fee in a separate savings account earmarked for taxes

sections.content: Understanding your tax exposure is only part of the equation — finding enough profitable deals to make the math work is the other part.
